title: Mark 15
subtitle: The Crucifixion: Trial, Cross, Death, Burial

sections.header: Verses 1–20 — Before Pilate; Barabbas Released; The Soldiers Mock

heb.paragraph: He had Jesus flogged — ephragelōsen. Roman flogging (verberatio) used a flagellum — a whip with leather thongs embedded with bone or metal fragments. It was used as pre-crucifixion torture to weaken the condemned. Isaiah 53:5 (“by his wounds we are healed”) refers to exactly this punishment.

tl.name: Good Friday — Morning trials before Pilate
tl.text: Very early Friday morning: Sanhedrin decision, transfer to Pilate, Barabbas exchange, flogging, and soldiers’ mocking. Crucifixion begins at 9am.

refs.note: He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth — the silent Servant who “gave no answer” (v.4-5).
The punishment that brought us peace was on him; by his wounds we are healed — the flogging of v.15 fulfils this text.
All who see me mock me; they hurl insults, shaking their heads — the mockery of the Passion psalm begins here.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "Are you the king of the Jews?" — the charge is political: claiming royal status is sedition against Caesar. Marcus notes the bitter irony: Jesus will be executed under a charge that is simultaneously false (he never led an armed revolt) and true (he is the Davidic king). The INRI placard makes the irony permanent and public. Marcus argues that Pilate's triple affirmation of Jesus' innocence (15:10, 14, "Why? What crime has he committed?") functions as a Roman legal testimony to the injustice of the execution.

Marcus: The Barabbas exchange is the Passion narrative's clearest enacted substitution: the guilty goes free; the innocent is condemned. Marcus notes that the name Barabbas (bar Abba — son of the father) may carry deliberate irony: the "son of the father" who is released is replaced by the Son of the Father who is executed. Whether or not the irony was intended in the historical event, Mark's narrative deploys it — the name is given without explanation, as if its resonance would be immediately apparent.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: Pilate is characterized through a sequence of questions and reactions rather than through direct evaluation. He asks the right questions ("What crime has he committed?"); he perceives the real motive ("he knew it was out of self-interest"); he wants to release Jesus (15:9-11); and he capitulates to the crowd. Rhoads argues that Pilate is Mark's most fully developed portrait of moral failure: not ignorance, not malice, but the preference for social safety over just action. He is the narrative's mirror of Herod Antipas (6:26).

Rhoads: The soldiers' mock coronation is the Gospel's most sustained sustained enactment of irony. Purple robe, crown of thorns, homage ("Hail, King of the Jews!"), striking, spitting, kneeling — each gesture of mockery performs the truth it intends to deny. Rhoads argues that Mark's narrator presents this scene without evaluative comment, trusting the reader to supply the irony. The soldiers unknowingly crown, robes, and enthrone the actual king. The mockery is the coronation.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The Roman trial before Pilate is, for Chrysostom, a study in the cowardice of power. Pilate knows Jesus is innocent (v14: "What crime has he committed?") and fears the crowd. The choice of Barabbas over Jesus is, for Origen, a figure charged with substitutionary theology: the genuinely guilty man is released; the genuinely innocent man is condemned. Bede notes the irony of the soldiers' mock homage — the purple robe, the crown of thorns, "Hail, King of the Jews" — as the truth spoken in derision: he is the king, the crown is his, the homage is due. The mockery is the inadvertent liturgy of those who do not know what they are doing.

The crucifixion narrative in Mark is, for Chrysostom, the most compressed of the four Gospels — there is no word from the cross except the cry of dereliction. Origen devotes extensive reflection to "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Psalm 22:1): the question is not theological despair but the opening of a psalm that ends in vindication (Psalm 22:24-31). The tearing of the Temple curtain (v38) is, for Ambrose, the simultaneous end of the old covenant economy and the opening of access to the holy of holies for all people. The centurion's confession "Surely this man was the Son of God!" is, for Theophylact, the Gentile world's first act of faith — the cross itself is the first proclamation.

You have said so — the response is neither affirmation nor denial but a deliberate deflection: the title “king of the Jews” is Pilate’s Roman frame; Jesus will not use it of himself but neither will he deny its truth.

Wanting to satisfy the crowd — the political calculation that condemns Jesus. Pilate knows Jesus is innocent (v.10, 14) and condemns him for crowd management. The same motivation that killed John the Baptist (6:26) kills Jesus.

Calvin: The Barabbas exchange is the clearest image in the Gospels of substitutionary release. Barabbas’s freedom is the direct result of Jesus’s condemnation. Calvin uses this to explain justification: the guilty go free because the innocent bears the punishment.

Jesus made no reply, and Pilate was amazed — Calvin: the silence fulfils Isa 53:7. Jesus’s silence is not passivity but the active submission of the Servant who has chosen to bear what is being laid on him.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: King of the Jews — the charge is political: claiming to be king is sedition against Rome. The accusation is simultaneously false (Jesus never led an armed rebellion) and true (he is the Davidic king). Pilate’s placard (v.26) will make the charge permanent: King of the Jews.

Flogged (ephragelōsen) — verberatio was so severe that victims sometimes died from it. The pre-crucifixion flogging guaranteed rapid death on the cross. The medical evidence for the physical devastation of Roman flogging supports the narrative’s plausibility.

hist.context: Pilate’s trial of Jesus is a study in political cowardice that mirrors Herod’s in chapter 6: both men know the prisoner is innocent, both protect him briefly, both capitulate to external pressure. Barabbas is the paradigm of substitutionary atonement: the guilty party goes free while the innocent one is condemned. The soldiers’ mock coronation (purple robe, crown of thorns, “Hail, king!”) is an unwitting enactment of the truth.

sections.header: Verses 21–41 — The Crucifixion; The Dereliction; The Curtain Torn

heb.gloss: My God my God why have you forsaken me (Aramaic)
heb.paragraph: The last Aramaic words preserved in Mark — the cry of Ps 22:1, spoken by the dying Jesus. This is not despair but the prayer of the righteous sufferer: Psalm 22 opens with abandonment and ends with vindication (Ps 22:24-31). Jesus prays the whole psalm from its opening cry.

tl.name: Good Friday — Crucifixion; Death 3pm; Burial
tl.text: 9am: crucified. Noon–3pm: three hours of darkness. 3pm: the dereliction cry and death. Temple veil torn. Evening: burial before Sabbath.

refs.note: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — the Passion psalm that structures the crucifixion narrative: lots for clothing (Ps 22:18), the taunts (Ps 22:7-8), the cry (Ps 22:1), the vindication (Ps 22:24-31).
He was numbered with the transgressors — fulfilled in v.27: crucified between two rebels.
The Temple veil separating the Holy of Holies — torn from top to bottom at the moment of death.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani" — the Aramaic cry preserved verbatim is the most emotionally raw moment in the Gospel. Marcus argues it is not despair but prayer: Psalm 22 opens with abandonment and ends with vindication and worldwide praise (Ps 22:22-31). To pray the opening of Ps 22 is to invoke the whole psalm, including its ending. The dereliction is real — not theatrical — but it is the dereliction of the Righteous Sufferer who trusts in divine rescue even from the depths of God-forsakenness.

Marcus: The simultaneous tearing of the Temple veil (katapetasma) and the centurion's confession is Mark's most compressed theological statement. Marcus argues the two events are causally related in Mark's narrative logic: the veil that separated the Holy of Holies (God's dwelling) from the outer courts is torn, and immediately a Gentile — standing on the threshold of Gentile access to God — confesses the truth. The tearing opens the access; the confession is the first act of the newly opened communion.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The crucifixion narrative is the most scripturally dense passage in Mark. Rhoads identifies seven allusions to Psalm 22 in seven verses (15:24-36): dividing garments (Ps 22:18), mocking (Ps 22:7-8), the cry (Ps 22:1), the vinegar (Ps 22:15), the dogs surrounding (Ps 22:16). The narrator does not cite Psalm 22 directly — the reader must recognise the allusions. This positions the ideal reader as one steeped in the psalms: the crucifixion's meaning is revealed only to those who can hear the scriptural resonance.

Rhoads: The centurion's confession closes the bracket opened in 1:1. The Gospel begins with the narrator's declaration ("the Son of God"); it ends with a human character — a Gentile, a soldier, an agent of the execution — making the same declaration. Rhoads argues this is the narrative's final irony: the people who should have recognized Jesus (the religious establishment, the disciples) did not; the pagan executioner who sees him die does. The title "Son of God" frames the entire Gospel; the last human voice to say it is the least expected.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Simon of Cyrene is, for Origen, the first person explicitly named in Mark as carrying the cross — Chrysostom notes that his sons Alexander and Rufus are named as known to Mark's community, suggesting they became believers. The offer of wine mixed with myrrh (v23) which Jesus refuses is, for Bede, a deliberate acceptance of the full experience of death without analgesic — he does not diminish the suffering. The mocking at the cross ("Come down from the cross and save yourself!", v30) is, for Ambrose, the supreme temptation in the passion narrative: save yourself, or save others. The cross is the answer.

The three hours of darkness (v33) are, for Chrysostom, the creation's mourning for its Creator — an echo of the Egyptian plague of darkness and a sign that cosmic order is disrupted when the light of the world is extinguished. The cry of dereliction in Aramaic (v34) is preserved by Mark in the original language, which Origen says is because the sound carries the full weight of the moment. Joseph of Arimathea is, for Bede, the representative of the hidden believers — members of the establishment who had not been able to declare themselves, now emboldened by the cross itself.

Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani — the cry of Ps 22:1 from the cross. The Son of God experiencing genuine God-forsakenness — the consequence of bearing sin that separates from God. This is the atonement’s deepest cost: not merely physical death but the experience of covenant rupture that sin produces.

The torn veil and the centurion’s confession occur simultaneously at the moment of death. The veil’s tearing (from top to bottom: divine action) removes the barrier to God’s presence. The centurion’s confession names who is responsible: the Son of God.

My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — Calvin: the dereliction is genuine and necessary. The Son experiences the God-forsakenness that sin deserves, so that sinners do not have to. The cry is not the absence of faith but its most extreme expression: prayer in the darkness without the assurance of answer.

The veil torn from top to bottom — Calvin: the downward direction (“from top to bottom”) indicates divine action. The ceremonial law’s barrier between humanity and God’s immediate presence is removed not by human effort but by the death of the Mediator.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Why have you forsaken me (egkatelipes) — the LXX of Ps 22:1. The perfect tense in the Greek: “you have forsaken me.” The experience of abandonment is being spoken from within it. Psalm 22 proceeds from this opening to a final affirmation of divine rescue (Ps 22:24-31); Jesus prays the whole psalm from its beginning.

Pilate was surprised that he was already dead — victims of crucifixion typically survived 1-3 days. Jesus died after approximately 6 hours — likely due to the severity of the flogging. Pilate’s verification through the centurion is an early historical attestation against later claims of a swoon.

hist.context: The crucifixion account is structured around three sets of three: three inscriptions (Title, King of the Jews, Messiah); three groups of mockers (passers-by, chief priests, the crucified); and the three hours of darkness (noon to three). The moment of death is framed by two tearing events: the darkness and the torn veil. The centurion’s confession (“Son of God”) is the Gospel’s climax — the first human being to say what the opening verse (1:1) declared.

sections.header: Verses 42–47 — The Burial: Joseph of Arimathea

heb.paragraph: He had Jesus flogged — ephragelōsen. Roman flogging (verberatio) used a flagellum — a whip with leather thongs embedded with bone or metal fragments. It was used as pre-crucifixion torture to weaken the condemned. Isaiah 53:5 (“by his wounds we are healed”) refers to exactly this punishment.

tl.name: Good Friday — Morning trials before Pilate
tl.text: Very early Friday morning: Sanhedrin decision, transfer to Pilate, Barabbas exchange, flogging, and soldiers’ mocking. Crucifixion begins at 9am.

refs.note: He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth — the silent Servant who “gave no answer” (v.4-5).
The punishment that brought us peace was on him; by his wounds we are healed — the flogging of v.15 fulfils this text.
All who see me mock me; they hurl insults, shaking their heads — the mockery of the Passion psalm begins here.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: On 47 — The Burial: Joseph of Arimathea: omposition" well attested in Second Temple Jewish midrash. The Qumran community (1QS 8:12-14) also applied Isa 40:3 to their own wilderness mission, suggesting that this text was a live messianic-preparatory image in Jesus's era.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: On 47 — The Burial: Joseph of Arimathea: iah, the Son of God." The narrator tells the reader what no character in the story yet knows. This creates a sustained dramatic irony — the audience watches characters struggle with a question (who is Jesus?) whose answer has been given in verse 1.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Patristic commentary on 47 — The Burial: Joseph of Arimathea:  action. The quotation from Isaiah and Malachi (vv.2-3) in one breath is deliberate: Aquinas, following Bede, says it shows the two offices of the Forerunner — as the messenger of the covenant (Malachi) and as the voice in the wilderness (Isaiah). The prophecy precedes the person; the word precedes the Word.

Calvin: On 47 — The Burial: Joseph of Arimathea: ng — because the gospel is primarily the announcement of salvation. The prophetic testimony grounds it in ancient covenant fulfilment, not new invention. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 47 — The Burial: Joseph of Arimathea: ingle event at the end. The wild animals are unique to Mark — possibly Edenic restoration imagery or Markan geographical realism about the desert terrain. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Pilate’s trial of Jesus is a study in political cowardice that mirrors Herod’s in chapter 6: both men know the prisoner is innocent, both protect him briefly, both capitulate to external pressure. Barabbas is the paradigm of substitutionary atonement: the guilty party goes free while the innocent one is condemned. The soldiers’ mock coronation (purple robe, crown of thorns, “Hail, king!”) is an unwitting enactment of the truth.

ppl.name: Pontius Pilate
ppl.text: Pilate’s portrait in Mark follows the Herod pattern: knowledge of innocence, temporary protection, capitulation to pressure. His three-part question-sequence (v.2, 4, 9, 12) and final capitulation structure the trial as a moral failure rather than a legal one.

ppl.name: The centurion
ppl.role: Unnamed Roman officer; first human confessor of the Son of God
ppl.text: His confession in 15:39 closes the bracket opened in 1:1. Mark’s Gospel begins with the narrator identifying Jesus as Son of God; it ends with a pagan Roman soldier identifying him the same way, at the cross.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?" (which means "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?")ESV"Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?" which means, "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?"KJV"Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani?" which is, being interpreted, My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?NASB"Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?" which is translated, "My God, My God, why have you forsaken Me?"NLT"Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?" which means "My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?"

src.title: Josephus, Jewish War 5.212-214
src.quote: Josephus describes the Temple veil as 80 cubits high and 24 wide, made of fine linen in four colours — a babylonian curtain of enormous scale.
src.note: Confirms the physical reality of the veil and helps the reader understand the dramatic impact of its tearing.

src.title: Tacitus, Annals 15.44
src.quote: Tacitus confirms the execution of Christ under Pontius Pilate during the reign of Tiberius — the earliest pagan attestation of the crucifixion.
src.note: One of the strongest non-Christian sources confirming the historical fact of Jesus’s execution under Pilate.

rec.text: Anselm’s satisfaction theory of atonement is built on the logic of the cross: the infinite offense of sin against God required an infinite satisfaction, possible only from one who was both human (to represent humanity) and divine (to offer infinite value).

Moltmann argued that the cry of dereliction (15:34) reveals that God is not immune to suffering. The cross is the event in which God takes the God-forsakenness of human experience into the divine life itself. The Trinity is implicated in the crucifixion.

rows.label: Two Tearing Events: Darkness and Veil

hebtext.note: He had Jesus flogged — ephragelōsen. Roman flogging (verberatio) used a flagellum — a whip with leather thongs embedded with bone or metal fragments. It was used as pre-crucifixion torture to weaken the condemned. Isaiah 53:5 (“by his wounds we are healed”) refers to exactly this punishment.

hebtext.gloss: My God my God why have you forsaken me (Aramaic)
hebtext.note: The last Aramaic words preserved in Mark — the cry of Ps 22:1, spoken by the dying Jesus. This is not despair but the prayer of the righteous sufferer: Psalm 22 opens with abandonment and ends with vindication (Ps 22:24-31). Jesus prays the whole psalm from its opening cry.

He had Jesus flogged — ephragelōsen. Roman flogging (verberatio) used a flagellum — a whip with leather thongs embedded with bone or metal fragments. It was used as pre-crucifixion torture to weaken the condemned. Isaiah 53:5 (“by his wounds we are healed”) refers to exactly this punishment.

tx.title: "The third hour" vs "the sixth hour" — conflict with John
tx.content: Mark 15:25 says Jesus was crucified at "the third hour" (9am); John 19:14 says it was "about the sixth hour" (noon) when Pilate handed him over. No MS tradition resolves the discrepancy.
tx.note: The most common explanations: (1) different hour-counting systems; (2) John's theological symbolism (sixth hour = when the Passover lambs begin to be slaughtered); (3) scribal adjustment in one Gospel to match the other. The NA28 accepts both readings as original to their respective Gospels.

tx.title: "Eloi, Eloi" or "Eli, Eli" — Aramaic vs Hebrew form
tx.content: Mark readsElōi, Elōi(Aramaic: my God, my God); Matthew 27:46 readsĒli, Ēli(Hebrew). Some MSS of Mark harmonise toward Matthew's Hebrew form.
tx.note: Mark's Aramaic form (Eloi) is original to Mark and explains why bystanders thought he was calling Elijah — the name sounds more like Elijah in the Aramaic. Matthew's Hebrew form makes the misunderstanding less explicable. No copyist problem; both forms preserve authentic tradition.

debate.title: What did the torn veil signify?

positions.name: Access to God
positions.proponents: The veil that separated the Holy of Holies from the Holy Place — the barrier to the divine presence — was torn, signifying that through Christ’s death direct access to God is now available (Heb 9:8-12).
positions.argument: Access to the Most Holy Place

positions.name: Temple judgment
positions.proponents: The tearing is a sign of God’s judgment on the Temple system, anticipating its physical destruction in AD 70.

themes.note: Mark 15 is the atonement chapter: the ransom for many (10:45) is paid. The crucifixion is structured by Psalm 22, the torn veil opens access to God, and the centurion’s confession names what has been accomplished.

coaching.tip: Barabbas—'son of the father'—is released; the true Son of the Father is condemned. The crowd chooses a murderer over the Messiah. Mark's irony: substitution in reverse.

'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (v. 34). Psalm 22's opening cry. The centurion sees Jesus die and declares: 'Surely this man was the Son of God!' (v. 39). A Gentile soldier gets what Israel's leaders missed.

prayer_prompt: As the centurion at the cross declares 'Surely this man was the Son of God,' consider asking God what it was about the way Jesus died that revealed who He was — and what your own suffering might reveal about your faith.
